title: Go WordPress Crawler: Advanced Structural Improvements
subtitle: A Deep Dive into Enhanced Web Scraping Architecture and Configuration
excerpt: A Deep Dive into Enhanced Web Scraping Architecture and Configuration

content.html: A Deep Dive into Enhanced Web Scraping Architecture and Configuration

The go-wordpress project is a sophisticated web scraping framework written in Go that specializes in extracting product data from WordPress-based e-commerce websites. The recent architectural changes represent a significant evolution in the project’s capabilities, introducing a more robust and flexible data model, improved separation of concerns, and enhanced configuration management. This article provides a comprehensive analysis of these changes, their implications, and their benefits to the development workflow.

content.alt: Go WordPress Crawler: Advanced Structural Improvements

content.html: The foundation of the enhanced data model includes two complementary product structures:

items: Product: Raw data directly extracted from HTML. All fields are stored as strings, preserving the original format from the website. This includes the title, price, link, image URL, and description exactly as they appear in the HTML.

ProductClean: Normalized and validated data with type safety. The price field is converted from a string to int64, ensuring type consistency and enabling mathematical operations. Other fields maintain their string types, but with cleaned and trimmed values.

content.alt: Go WordPress Crawler: Advanced Structural Improvements

content.html: This dual-structure approach provides several benefits:

items: Data Integrity: Original raw data is preserved in the Product struct, enabling audit trails and debugging
Type Safety: The ProductClean struct ensures prices are numeric, preventing type errors downstream
Flexibility: Consumers can choose between raw or cleaned data based on their needs
Validation: The cleaning process validates data quality before it reaches production systems

content.html: Categories represent product collections on an e-commerce site. Each category contains metadata and dual product collections that mirror the Product/ProductClean pattern.
The Category structure includes:

items: Name: Human-readable category identifier (e.g., “پکیجها” — Packages in Persian)
Link: URL to the category listing page
Products: Slice of raw Product structs extracted from the website
CleanedProducts: Slice of ProductClean structs after validation and normalization

content.html: This dual-collection approach enables the system to preserve original data while providing validated, normalized data for consumption.
The Website structure serves as the primary configuration container for the crawler. It defines all necessary parameters for scraping a specific website:

content.code:
type Website struct {
    Domain string
    Categories []Category
    StartURLs []string `json:"start_urls"`
    ProductList ProductListConfig `json:"product_list"`
    Pagination *PaginationConfig `json:"pagination,omitempty"`
    ProductDetail ProductDetailConfig `json:"product_detail"`
}

content.html: Key components:

items: Domain: The target website domain (e.g., “badomjip.com”) used for allowed domain validation
Categories: Array of category objects with links to product listing pages
StartURLs: Array of initial URLs to begin crawling (optional, categories provide the primary entry points)
ProductList: Configuration for extracting products from list pages
Pagination: Configuration for handling multi-page results (optional, indicated by pointer type)
ProductDetail: Configuration for extracting product descriptions from detail pages

content.html: The FieldConfig structure defines how to extract a specific piece of data from HTML:

items: Selector: CSS selector for targeting elements (e.g., “h3.wd-entities-title a”)
Attr: Attribute name if extracting an attribute value. An empty string means extract text content

content.html: This generic approach decouples the crawler logic from website-specific HTML structures. Whether you need to extract text content or an href attribute, the same FieldConfig structure handles both cases.
Example usage in extraction:

content.code:
// Extract text content.
Title: e.ChildText(s.web.ProductList.Fields["title"].Selector)

content.html: Defines how products are extracted from listing pages:

items: Item: CSS selector matching each product element on the listing page
Fields: Map of field names (title, price, link, image) to their extraction configurations

content.html: This map-based approach allows flexible field extraction without modifying code. For example, the configuration can specify that product titles are found at “h3.wd-entities-title a” and prices at “span.price”. The crawler dynamically applies these selectors to each product item.
Configuration example:
Handles multi-page product listings:

items: Next: CSS selector for the “next page” link element

content.html: When the crawler encounters a page with products, it looks for a “next” link using this selector. When found, the Gocolly collector automatically visits the next page and continues extraction. This eliminates the need for hardcoded pagination logic and makes the crawler adaptable to different pagination schemes.
How it works:
Specifies how to extract detailed product information from individual product pages:

Currently, it defines the Description field configuration. This structure can be extended to include other details like specifications, reviews, availability information, or ratings without modifying the core crawler logic.
The Service struct encapsulates the crawler logic, managing state through three fields:

items: web: Pointer to the Website configuration containing all extraction rules
logger: Structured logger (zap) for comprehensive logging
mu: Mutex for thread-safe concurrent operations during async description fetching

content.html: This design follows Go idioms and enables graceful concurrent scraping of product pages while maintaining data integrity.

content.text: 3.2 The Run Method: Orchestrating the Pipeline

content.html: The Run method orchestrates the entire crawling workflow with a clear three-step pipeline:
The workflow:

items: ListProducts: Fetches all products from category listing pages with pagination support
FetchDescriptions: Asynchronously retrieves product descriptions from individual product pages
cleanUP: Validates and normalizes all extracted data

content.html: This separation enables:

items: Monitoring: Track which step fails and log detailed errors
Optimization: Each step can be optimized independently
Reusability: Steps can be called in different orders or combinations
Testability: Each step can be tested in isolation

content.text: 3.3 ListProducts Method: Extraction with Dynamic Selectors

content.html: This method iterates through configured categories and creates a Gocolly collector for each:
Key features:

items: Dynamic selector-based extraction: Uses FieldConfig to determine where to find each field
Support for text and attribute extraction: Handles both content extraction and attribute parsing
Automatic pagination: Follows “next” links without manual intervention
Validation: Ensures product links exist before storing products
Detailed logging: Logs skipped products for debugging

content.text: 3.4 FetchDescriptions Method: Concurrent Scraping with Rate Limiting

content.html: This asynchronous method demonstrates advanced concurrency patterns essential for production web scraping:
go
Advanced features:

items: Async(true): Enables concurrent requests instead of sequential processing
LimitRule: Controls parallelism to 4 concurrent requests with 800ms delay between them
Prevents overwhelming target servers
Respects robots.txt conventions
Reduces the risk of IP blocking
Context objects: Pass product indices to callbacks for accurate data mapping without relying on shared state
Mutex protection: Ensures thread-safe modification of concurrent slice updates
Error handling: Logs failures without stopping the entire process
Wait(): Blocks until all async requests complete

content.html: The cleanUP method performs a comprehensive data transformation:
Comprehensive data transformation:

items: HTML Tag Removal: Uses regex <[^>]*> to strip HTML tags from titles and descriptions, preserving clean text content
Price Parsing: A sophisticated multi-step process:
Removes currency text (e.g., “تومان”)
Removes formatting characters (commas, spaces)
Extracts only digits using regex
Converts Persian digits to ASCII
Parses to int64
Persian Digit Conversion: Converts Persian numerals (۰-۹) to ASCII digits (0–9)

content.html: This is crucial for handling Iranian e-commerce sites

items: Type Conversion: Converts price strings to int64, providing type safety and enabling numerical operations

content.html: Benefits:

items: Ensures data consistency across different website formats
Enables downstream systems to rely on predictable data types
Handles international number systems transparently
Validates data quality before it reaches production

content.html: The test suite includes integration tests demonstrating real-world crawler functionality against actual websites:
Test coverage:

items: Tests the crawler against badomjip.com, a Persian e-commerce site
Validates that the crawler successfully loads the configuration
Confirms the complete pipeline runs without errors
Produces cleaned products with non-zero prices and populated fields
Confirms the crawler correctly handles Persian text and currency formats

content.html: Test the crawler against Hosseinibrothers. ir, another Persian e-commerce platform with a different HTML structure. This test ensures the configuration-driven approach handles diverse website layouts without code changes. It validates the same assertions as TestBadomjip, confirming crawler robustness across different target sites.
Why two tests?

items: Structural diversity: Different websites have different HTML structures
Configuration validation: Demonstrates that configurations are portable
Regression prevention: Ensures updates don’t break existing functionality
Real-world proof: Actual tests against live websites provide confidence

content.html: The badomjip.json configuration demonstrates selector-based extraction for a WordPress WooCommerce store:
Configuration breakdown:

items: Product Item Selector (div.product-grid-item): Targets individual product cards
Title Extraction (h3.wd-entities-title a): Retrieved as text content
Price Extraction (span.price): Obtained as text content, later parsed to an integer
Link Extraction (a.product-image-link, attr: href): Retrieved from href attribute
Image Extraction (a.product-image-link img, attr: src): Retrieved from the src attribute
Pagination (a.next.page-numbers): Automatically follows next page links
Description (Multiple selectors): Handles both short and detailed descriptions

content.html: This configuration handles a completely different HTML structure:
Configuration breakdown:

items: Product Item Selector (div.js-product-miniature): Different class names
Title and Link (a.stsb_mini_product_name): Same selector, different extraction methods
Price (div.stsb_pm_price): Different structure than WooCommerce
Image (img.stsb_pm_image): Custom image class
Multiple Categories: Demonstrates support for category-specific links
Pagination (a[rel='next']): Uses attribute selector instead of class-based selector
Description: Handles multiple selector alternatives with comma separation

content.alt: Go WordPress Crawler: Advanced Structural Improvements

content.html: Despite these differences, both sites are fully supported through configuration alone, without code changes.
Raw data extraction, validation, and cleaning are handled in separate methods, making code easier to test and maintain:

items: ListProducts(): Responsible only for HTML parsing and product extraction
FetchDescriptions(): Handles async description fetching
cleanUP(): Manages data validation and normalization

content.html: Each method has a single responsibility, following the Single Responsibility Principle. This makes the codebase:

items: Testable: Each step can be tested independently
Debuggable: Issues can be isolated to specific methods
Maintainable: Changes to one step don’t affect others
Reusable: Methods can be composed in different ways

content.html: New websites can be added with JSON configuration files only, without modifying the core crawler code:

items: No code compilation needed: Administrators can add new websites by creating JSON files
Dynamic selector support: Each website can have completely different HTML structures
Version control friendly: Configuration files can be versioned separately
A/B testing ready: Different configurations can be tested without deployment
Dramatically reduced effort: Supporting a new site takes minutes instead of days

content.html: This dramatically reduces the effort needed to support new e-commerce platforms.
The ProductClean struct with proper types (int64 for price) enables compile-time type checking:

items: Prevents runtime errors: Type mismatches caught at compile time
Enables math operations: int64 prices can be summed, averaged, compared
Database compatibility: Proper types map naturally to database schemas
API contracts: Clear type definitions for REST/gRPC endpoints
Reduces bugs: No more string-to-number conversion errors at runtime

content.html: The Persian digit conversion and handling of RTL scripts demonstrates built-in support for international e-commerce platforms:

items: Multi-language support: Handles Persian, Arabic, and other number systems
Currency flexibility: Removes currency text before parsing (تومان, etc.)
No additional configuration: Localization is built into the data cleaning
Extensible: Easy to add support for additional numeral systems

content.html: The implementation leverages Gocolly’s async capabilities with controlled parallelism:
Benefits:

items: Efficiency: Multiple product pages fetched simultaneously
Resource control: Limits prevent server overload and connection exhaustion
Ethical scraping: Delays between requests respect server limits
Scalability: Same code handles 10 products or 10,000
Production-ready: Rate limiting prevents IP blocking

content.html: Performance characteristics:

items: Sequential listing extraction: Fast, single-threaded
Parallel description fetching: 4 concurrent requests, 800ms delay = efficient crawling
Total crawl time reduced from (N * 800ms) to (ceil(N/4) * 800ms)

content.html: Integration with Zap Logger provides structured, efficient logging:

content.code:
s.logger.Info(
    "Visiting next page",
    zap.String("url", nextPage),
)

s.logger.Debug(
    "Product link is not valid",
    zap.Any("product", p),
)

s.logger.Warn(
    "Failed to visit product",
    zap.String("url", url),
    zap.Error(err),
)

content.html: Features:

items: Structured fields: Each log entry contains typed fields (not just strings)
Log levels: DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR for filtering
Performance: Allocates minimal memory for log entries
Monitoring: Easy to parse logs programmatically
Debugging: Context-rich logs help troubleshoot issues
Compliance: Audit trails for production systems

content.html: The architecture supports numerous extensions without breaking changes:

items: Additional Product Fields: The ProductDetailConfig can be extended to extract specifications, ratings, stock levels, etc.
Request Customization: Add headers configuration for sites requiring authentication or a custom User-Agent
JavaScript Rendering: Support for JavaScript-rendered content via Chrome DevTools Protocol
Proxy Support: Configuration-based proxy rotation for large-scale crawling
Error Recovery: Retry logic with exponential backoff for transient failures
Data Export: Built-in formats for CSV, JSON, and database export
Scheduling: Background job scheduling for periodic crawls

content.html: The design naturally supports these extensions because:

items: Configuration is a first-class citizen: New options can be added to JSON configs
Plugin pattern: New extraction types can be registered dynamically
Clean interfaces: Core crawler logic is decoupled from infrastructure
Go’s simplicity: Easy to add features without massive refactoring

content.html: The current implementation demonstrates production-ready practices:

items: ✅ Error handling: All operations check for errors
✅ Logging: Comprehensive structured logging for debugging
✅ Rate limiting: Respects target server constraints
✅ Concurrency safety: Mutexes protect shared state
✅ Configuration validation: Structures validated on load
✅ Testing: Integration tests against real websites
✅ Data validation: Cleanup ensures data quality
✅ Type safety: Proper types prevent runtime errors

content.html: What’s needed for production:

items: Database integration for storing products
Metrics collection (Prometheus/Grafana)
Health checks and alerting
Backup and recovery procedures
Rate limiting per category/domain
Cache layer for frequently accessed data

content.html: The go-wordpress project’s recent architectural changes represent a mature, production-ready approach to web scraping. By combining configuration-driven design with clean separation of concerns, comprehensive data validation, and robust concurrency handling, the framework achieves flexibility without sacrificing code quality.

items: The dual data model (Product/ProductClean) ensures data integrity while maintaining flexibility
Configuration-driven architecture enables support for diverse websites without code changes
Structured concurrency with rate limiting provides ethical, efficient scraping
Comprehensive data cleaning handles international formats and validations
Architectural clarity makes the codebase maintainable and extensible

content.html: Whether supporting Persian e-commerce platforms or any other WordPress-based site, the project demonstrates how thoughtful architectural decisions enable scalable, maintainable web scraping infrastructure. The framework isn’t just a one-off solution — it’s a platform for building production-grade data pipelines.
The separation of concerns, configuration-driven design, and proper handling of concurrency create a solid foundation that can grow with your needs, whether you’re crawling 100 products or 100,000.
